title: Get Ready for the Course
text:
// PRE-COURSE SETUP В В·В  иҜҫеүҚеҮҶеӨҮ




  01
  
    GitHub + Copilot
    
      GitHub is where your team's code lives вҖ” it's a deliverable. Copilot is GitHub's AI pair programmer,
      built into VS Code. It completes code, explains unfamiliar functions, and writes boilerplate so you can focus on the interesting parts.
    
    
      Create a free GitHub account at github.com
      Apply for the GitHub Education Pack at education.github.com/pack вҖ” use your @shanghaitech.edu.cn email. Approval takes 1вҖ“3 days and gives you Copilot for free.
      Install GitHub Copilot as a VS Code extension (step 5 вҖ” set up VS Code first if needed)
    
    вҶ’ github.com
    вҶ’ Education Pack
    Apply for the Education Pack as early as possible вҖ” approval is not instant.
  



  02
  
    OpenRouter
    
      OpenRouter is a single API gateway to 300+ AI models вҖ” Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude, and others.
      Your ESP32 will call OpenRouter to get your robot's AI responses.
      You use one API key, one endpoint, and can swap models with a one-line change.
    
    
      Create a free account at openrouter.ai
      Go to Settings вҶ’ API Keys and create a key вҖ” save it somewhere safe
      Add $5вҖ“10 of credit under Settings вҶ’ Credits вҖ” this will last the entire course
    
    вҶ’ openrouter.ai
    вҶ’ Browse models
    $5 is genuinely enough. Each LLM call costs fractions of a cent at the models we use.
  



  03
  
    Blender
    
      Blender is the 3D modeling tool you'll use to design your robot's enclosure вҖ” the physical body that makes it a product, not just a circuit on a table.
      It's free, open-source, and runs on any computer.
      You don't need to know it yet вҖ” we'll learn the basics together.
    
    
      Download and install Blender from blender.org/download вҖ” get the latest stable release
      Optional head start: watch the first 2 episodes of the Blender Guru donut tutorial вҖ” it covers the core interface in ~30 minutes
    
    вҶ’ blender.org/download
    Free forever. No account needed.
  



  04
  
    Claude Pro
    
      Claude Pro gives you access to Claude Code вҖ” Anthropic's agentic AI coding tool that runs in your terminal.
      It understands your entire codebase, writes firmware, explains ESP-IDF errors, and can refactor across multiple files at once.
      It's the fastest way to go from "I have no idea what this error means" to "it's working."
    
    
      Sign up at claude.ai вҖ” use any email
      Subscribe to Claude Pro ($20/month) вҖ” check if your institution has a discount or arrangement
      Install Claude Code in your terminal: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
    
    вҶ’ claude.ai
    вҶ’ Claude Code docs
    Requires Node.js. If you don't have it, install from nodejs.org first.
  



  05
  
    VS Code + PlatformIO + ESP-IDF
    
      VS Code is the editor. PlatformIO is the extension that handles ESP32 board config, library management, and flashing.
      ESP-IDF is Espressif's official development framework that PlatformIO uses under the hood.
      Together they turn your laptop into a proper embedded development workstation.
    
    
      Download and install VS Code from code.visualstudio.com
      Open VS Code вҶ’ Extensions (вҢҳвҮ§X) вҶ’ search PlatformIO IDE вҶ’ Install
      PlatformIO will install ESP-IDF automatically when you first open an ESP32 project
      While you're in Extensions, also install GitHub Copilot (from step 1)
    
    вҶ’ VS Code
    вҶ’ PlatformIO for VS Code
    вҶ’ ESP-IDF docs
    All free. The PlatformIO first-time install downloads ~500 MB of toolchains вҖ” do it on Wi-Fi.
  








TipAlready done? Try a hello world with Claude Code.



All five tools installed? Let Claude Code write your first firmware. HereвҖҷs how:
1. Create a PlatformIO project in VS Code
Open VS Code вҶ’ PlatformIO icon вҶ’ New Project вҶ’ Board: Espressif ESP32 Dev Module вҶ’ Framework: Arduino вҶ’ Finish.
2. Open Claude Code in the project folder
cd your-project-folder
claude
3. Tell Claude Code what you want
Write an Arduino sketch for ESP32 that blinks the built-in LED on pin 2
every 500ms. Put it in src/main.cpp. Then tell me the PlatformIO command
to build and upload it.
Claude Code writes the code, places it in the right file, and gives you the exact command to flash it. If something fails, paste the error back and ask it to fix it вҖ” that loop is the core skill youвҖҷll use all course.





  
  
    
  

  
  
    
      Don't panic. We're all figuring this out.
    
    
      Some of these tools didn't exist two years ago. One or two will probably change before the course starts.
      The documentation will have gaps. The AI will occasionally hallucinate something spectacular.
      This is not a sign you're doing it wrong вҖ” it's a sign you're at the frontier.
    
    
      Your instructors are learning too вҖ” we just have a head start of a few months.
      The best thing you can do is show up curious, set up the tools above, and be ready to break things on Day 1.
    
    
      Get ready to break things on Day 1.

title: Week 3 вҖ” Build
text:
// WEEK THREE В В·В  з¬¬дёүе‘Ё
  Build
  еҲ¶йҖ 
  
    The pieces exist. Now you make them into a product.
    Week 3 is the most intense week: soldering, firmware integration, 3D printing, and relentless user testing.
    By Friday you should have a working robot in an enclosure.

title: Week 3 вҖ” Build
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 3 you will be able to:

Integrate all hardware components (sensors, display, motor/speaker) into a unified system
Print and assemble a 3D-modeled enclosure
Run structured user tests and incorporate feedback into the product
Use Claude Code, Codex, or other AI tools to accelerate firmware development
Prepare a working demo for Week 4

title: Week 3 вҖ” Build
text:
Sessions
вҶ’ View period-by-period schedule

Session 5 вҖ” Monday В· Integration, User Testing & Prof.В Zhou
Guest lecture in the morning, then the hardest technical work of the course.
Agenda: - Guest lecture: Prof.В Zhou вҖ” Entrepreneurship, the startup ecosystem, and how to pitch to VCs & stakeholders (2 periods) - Full system integration lab: all components running together В· Shimon + Daniel - Debugging & stress-testing: run continuously for 10 minutes вҖ” what breaks? В· Shimon + Daniel - User testing: live sessions with people outside the class В· Shimon + Daniel - 3D printing: finalize enclosure design & submit print job В· Daniel






WarningThe integration wall



Most teams hit a wall here вҖ” something that worked separately breaks when combined. This is normal. Budget 50% of this session for debugging. The debugging is the learning.


Common integration issues to watch for:



Symptom
Likely cause




Device crashes on startup
Power rail canвҖҷt supply enough current


Display garbled
I2C address conflict


LLM calls time out
Wi-Fi and heavy compute conflict


Motor causes resets
Motor driver needs separate power






Session 6 вҖ” Wednesday В· Assembly, Iteration & Demo Rehearsal
Your robot gets a body. User feedback gets turned into fixes.






TipDesign for printability



The best enclosure is the one that actually prints. Design with 0.2mm layer height in mind, avoid overhangs greater than 45В°, and add clearance for USB ports and cables.


Agenda: - 3D print assembly & fit check В· hardware mounting В· Daniel - Enclosure refinement В· cable routing В· Daniel - User test debrief: what confused people? What delighted them? В· Shimon + Daniel - Rapid iteration: top-3 fixes from user testing В· Shimon + Daniel - Demo rehearsal: 2-minute run-through Г— 3, rotate presenters В· Shimon + Daniel - Open lab: last build time В· Daniel






NoteStructured user testing



A good user test takes 15 minutes per person. Give them a task, watch silently, take notes. DonвҖҷt explain or defend вҖ” just observe.


What to test: - Can a new user figure out how to interact with the robot without explanation? - Does the robotвҖҷs personality come through clearly? - Is the physical form coherent with the personality? - Does it work reliably for 5 minutes straight?


вң“ Week 3 Deliverables

Working robot in enclosure вҖ” fully integrated, reliable, battery/USB powered
User test report вҖ” 3вҖ“4 testers, observations, top changes made
Source code вҖ” commented, committed to GitHub
Wiring diagram вҖ” documented schematic
Demo-ready вҖ” 2-minute rehearsed demo of the robotвҖҷs key interaction

title: Week 3 вҖ” Build
text:
Resources for Week 3
Hardware Integration & Debugging

ESP-Claw hardware integration guide вҖ” multi-component examples and wiring reference
ESP32 Troubleshooting Guide вҖ” Random Nerd Tutorials вҖ” the most comprehensive community reference for upload errors, brownouts, and boot issues
Troubleshooting вҖ” Arduino ESP32 (Espressif official) вҖ” official troubleshooting for the Arduino-ESP32 framework

AI Coding Tools

Claude Code Overview вҖ” Anthropic вҖ” official Claude Code documentation
GitHub Copilot Quickstart вҖ” set up Copilot in VS Code in minutes
Get Started with GitHub Copilot in VS Code вҖ” MicrosoftвҖҷs official VS Code + Copilot tutorial

User Testing

Usability Testing 101 вҖ” Nielsen Norman Group вҖ” how to structure and run a usability test session
Qualitative Usability Testing: Study Guide вҖ” NNGroup вҖ” full lifecycle: planning, facilitation, synthesis


вҶҗ Week 2 вҖ” Design В |В  Week 4 вҖ” Pitch It вҶ’

title: Week 1 вҖ” Conceive
text:
// WEEK ONE В В·В  з¬¬дёҖе‘Ё
  Conceive
  жһ„жҖқ
  
    Before you touch a single component, you need to understand the person your robot will serve.
    Week 1 is about listening, asking, and imagining вҖ” turning a vague idea into a focused concept.

title: Week 1 вҖ” Conceive
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 1 you will be able to:

Conduct basic user research interviews and synthesize findings
Define a target user and a genuine unmet need
Articulate the personality and purpose of your robot in a one-page concept brief
Sketch interaction scenarios and rough form factors
Present your concept for peer critique and iteration

title: Week 1 вҖ” Conceive
text:
Sessions
вҶ’ View period-by-period schedule

Session 1 вҖ” Monday В· Kick-off, Character Design & Concept Brief
The course begins. Guest lecturer JunJun Shi leads the afternoon вҖ” students leave with a team, a concept direction, and a clear sense of what robot personality means.
Agenda: - Course overview and grading (Pass / No Pass) В· жўҒдҝҠзқҝ - Embedded systems & IoT fundamentals В· meet the ESP32 В· жўҒдҝҠзқҝ - Guest lecture: JunJun Shi вҖ” вҖңEmpathy as the starting point for designing robot personalityвҖқ В· character design workshop with sketching & LLM generative tools (2 periods) - Brief poster session вҖ” all teams present their robot concept (3 min each) - Industrial design intro: form follows function В· sketching В· Daniel



Session 2 вҖ” Wednesday В· ESP32, Firmware & Ideation
Hands-on from the start. By the end of the session your board is talking to an LLM.






TipDesign Thinking in action



The goal of user research is not to confirm your assumptions вҖ” itвҖҷs to break them. The most interesting insights come from unexpected answers.


Agenda: - LLMs & conversational AI fundamentals В· жўҒдҝҠзқҝ - ESP-Claw framework overview В· жўҒдҝҠзқҝ - ESP32 first contact: hardware setup, GPIO, breadboard wiring В· Shimon + Daniel - Firmware lab: Hello World вҖ” display output & LED blink В· Shimon + Daniel - Robot concept ideation workshop: user persona, target need В· Shimon + Daniel - Open studio: form exploration & sketching В· Daniel

title: Week 1 вҖ” Conceive
text:
The Robot Personality Brief
This is the key output of Week 1. It answers these questions in one page:







Question
What to define




Who is it for?
Specific user + context


What problem does it solve?
The core unmet need


What does it feel like to interact with?
Personality adjectives (3вҖ“5)


What does it say / how does it speak?
Tone, language, style


What does it look like?
Sketch or moodboard


What does it not do?
Explicit non-goals









ImportantKeep it on one page



The constraint is intentional. If you canвҖҷt summarize your concept in one page, you donвҖҷt have a concept yet вҖ” you have a wish list.




вң“ Week 1 Deliverables

User Persona вҖ” one documented user with a real need (from your interview)
Robot Concept Brief вҖ” 1-page: user, need, personality, rough form factor
3 concept sketches вҖ” rough drawings, one per team member
Team roles defined вҖ” who leads design, firmware, fabrication, pitch

title: Week 1 вҖ” Conceive
text:
Resources for Week 1
Design Thinking & User Research

Stanford d.school Design Thinking Bootcamp Bootleg вҖ” the canonical guide to empathy and concept work
Interview for Empathy вҖ” Stanford d.school method card вҖ” one-page field-ready guide for empathy interviews
User Interviews 101 вҖ” Nielsen Norman Group вҖ” planning, conducting, and extracting insight from user interviews
IDEO Human-Centered Design toolkit вҖ” broader design research methods

Robot Character & Personality

Almost Human: How to Write Robot and AI Characters вҖ” personality traits, speech patterns, and belief systems for AI characters
Designing Personas for Expressive Robots вҖ” ACM THRI вҖ” peer-reviewed research on building robot personas through speech, color, and movement

Getting Started with ESP32

Getting Started вҖ” Arduino ESP32 (Espressif official) вҖ” easiest on-ramp for students new to ESP32
Getting Started with ESP32 вҖ” Random Nerd Tutorials вҖ” step-by-step setup, pinout, and first sketch
ESP-Claw documentation вҖ” start getting familiar early


Next: Week 2 вҖ” Design вҶ’

title: MAKER-X
text:
Summer 2026 В В·В  ShanghaiTech University
    
    
    
      School of Creativity & Art (SCA) В В·В  School of Information Science & Technology (SIST) В В·В  School of Entrepreneurship and Management (SEM) В В·В  Kedao College
    
    
    
      You don't just build a robot вҖ” you conceive the idea,
      design its personality, build the electronics, and
      pitch it.
      Four weeks. One team. One product you own end-to-end.
    
    
      дҪ дёҚеҸӘжҳҜеңЁеҲ¶йҖ жңәеҷЁдәәвҖ”вҖ”дҪ иҰҒжһ„жҖқеҲӣж„ҸгҖҒи®ҫи®ЎдёӘжҖ§гҖҒжҗӯе»әз”өи·ҜпјҢжңҖз»Ҳзҷ»еҸ°еұ•зӨәгҖӮ

title: MAKER-X
text:
The Mission В· иҜҫзЁӢдҪҝе‘Ҫ
Maker-X is an intensive 4-week project course where cross-disciplinary teams build AI-powered robotic companions from scratch вҖ” hardware, firmware, personality, and pitch. Instruction in Chinese & English.



01 Conceive
Identify a real user and a genuine need. Define the personality and purpose of your robot before touching any hardware.




02 Design
Shape, sound, language, expression вҖ” every detail of the robotвҖҷs soul. Prototype rapidly and iterate without fear.




03 Build
Solder, code, 3D-print, and integrate. Electronics, firmware, enclosure вҖ” one complete, working product.




04 Pitch It вҳ…
Present your product to real university guests. A working demo, a poster, a story вҖ” your public debut.

title: MAKER-X
text:
Course Objectives В· иҜҫзЁӢзӣ®ж Ү
Maker-X is about more than building a robot. ItвҖҷs about becoming someone who can think, collaborate, and create at the intersection of technology and design.



рҹӨқ Cross-discipline Collaboration
Work in teams spanning engineering, design, and beyond. Learn to communicate across disciplines and ship something greater than the sum of its parts.




рҹ§  Confidence with AI & LLMs
Go from curious to capable. YouвҖҷll integrate large language models into real hardware вҖ” learning to prompt, tune, and deploy AI as a core part of a product.




рҹҺЁ Design Thinking in Practice
Empathy, creativity, rapid prototyping, iteration, and communication вҖ” not as theory, but as daily practice. The product is the proof.

title: MAKER-X
text:
Who Should Join В· жӢӣз”ҹеҜ№иұЎ



рҹҺ“ All ShanghaiTech Students
The course is open to all students across all schools and majors. No prior experience or knowledge in programming, design, robotics, or AI is needed.




рҹҢҗ Diversified Teams
We organize groups where different skills and backgrounds complement each other вҖ” so every student can contribute meaningfully and learn most effectively.

The course meets twice a week (Monday + Wednesday), with 6 periods per session вҖ” enough time to go deep, not enough time to hesitate. вҶ’ View full class schedule

title: Week 2 вҖ” Design
text:
// WEEK TWO В В·В  з¬¬дәҢе‘Ё
  Design
  и®ҫи®Ў
  
    You have a concept. Now make it real вҖ” at low fidelity first, then higher.
    Week 2 is about rapid prototyping and fearless iteration:
    electronics on a breadboard, firmware running hello-world, and a 3D sketch of the body.

title: Week 2 вҖ” Design
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 2 you will be able to:

Wire a basic ESP32 circuit with a display or sensor
Flash firmware using the ESP-Claw framework
Send your first message to an LLM via OpenRouter and receive a response
Sketch and prototype the robotвҖҷs physical form
Run a first user test (lo-fi prototype, real feedback)

title: Week 2 вҖ” Design
text:
Sessions
вҶ’ View period-by-period schedule

Session 3 вҖ” Monday В· Electronics, Firmware & LLM Integration
The hardware talks. By the end of the day your robot will be sending a prompt to an LLM and displaying the answer on screen.






Note



This is often the most intimidating session. DonвҖҷt worry вҖ” by the end of it your board will be blinking, displaying text, or responding to a button press. ThatвҖҷs real.


Agenda: - OpenRouter, APIs & LLM system architecture В· жўҒдҝҠзқҝ - MCP protocol В· cloud/LLM integration patterns В· жўҒдҝҠзқҝ - Electronics lab: wiring sensors, displays & actuators В· Shimon + Daniel - Firmware lab: first HTTP call to OpenRouter from ESP32, display reply В· Shimon + Daniel - Prompt engineering: write a system prompt that gives your robot its personality В· Shimon + Daniel - 3D modeling intro & enclosure concept sketches В· Daniel
Starter prompt template:
You are [robot name], a [personality adjective] AI companion
designed for [target user].

You respond in [language preference], in [tone] вҖ” no more than
2 sentences per reply. You never break character.

When someone says hello, you respond with your signature greeting.






TipWhy OpenRouter?



OpenRouter provides a single API that routes to any LLM вҖ” Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude, and others. You write one integration and can swap models with a single parameter change. No re-wiring required.





Session 4 вҖ” Wednesday В· Form Development & Espressif Guest Lecture
The physical form takes shape. Guest engineers from Espressif explain the full ESP32 ecosystem.
Agenda: - Design critique: prototype review & form iteration В· Daniel - Form development workshop: translating personality into shape В· Daniel - Guest lecture: Espressif engineers вҖ” ESP32 family & ecosystem deep dive, ESP-Claw tooling & partner integrations (2 periods) - Integration lab: hardware + firmware + LLM end-to-end В· Shimon + Daniel - Open studio: prototype build В· Daniel






ImportantLo-fi is the point



Paper prototypes arenвҖҷt вҖңnot done yet.вҖқ TheyвҖҷre a tool. The goal is to learn fast and cheaply before committing to 3D printing.




вң“ Week 2 Deliverables

Working hardware sketch вҖ” ESP32 + display showing robot name / first response
LLM integration demo вҖ” robot sends a prompt and displays the reply
3D sketch / paper prototype of the robotвҖҷs enclosure
Personality system prompt вҖ” documented, tested, iterable
User test notes вҖ” what you showed, who you showed it to, what you learned

title: Week 2 вҖ” Design
text:
Resources for Week 2
Firmware & Hardware

ESP-Claw Getting Started вҖ” official ESP-Claw docs and examples
PlatformIO + ESP-IDF setup вҖ” PlatformIOвҖҷs ESP-IDF framework integration guide
VS Code + PlatformIO + ESP32 setup вҖ” SunFounder вҖ” illustrated step-by-step guide for the recommended IDE setup

LLM Integration & Prompt Engineering

OpenRouter Quickstart вҖ” API key setup and first call in minutes
OpenRouter Models list & pricing вҖ” compare Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude and others
Prompt Engineering Overview вҖ” Anthropic вҖ” structured guide to writing effective prompts
Keep Claude in Character вҖ” Anthropic вҖ” how to maintain robot personality consistency
Anthropic Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial вҖ” hands-on Jupyter notebook course from Anthropic

3D Modeling with Blender

Beginner Blender 5.0 Donut Tutorial вҖ” Blender Guru вҖ” the most-watched Blender beginner series, updated for 2025
Official Blender Tutorials вҖ” Blender.org вҖ” curated official tutorial index


вҶҗ Week 1 вҖ” Conceive В |В  Week 3 вҖ” Build вҶ’

title: Week 4 вҖ” Pitch It вҳ…
text:
// WEEK FOUR вҳ… В В·В  з¬¬еӣӣе‘Ё
  Pitch It
  еұ•зӨә
  
    You've built something real. Now tell its story.
    Week 4 is your public debut: a poster, a live demo, and a 5-minute presentation
    to university guests, instructors, and peers.

title: Week 4 вҖ” Pitch It вҳ…
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 4 you will be able to:

Communicate a productвҖҷs value clearly to a non-technical audience
Create a professional academic poster
Give a confident live demo under pressure
Synthesize 4 weeks of work into a coherent narrative
Receive and respond to critical questions gracefully

title: Week 4 вҖ” Pitch It вҳ…
text:
The Final Deliverables
Three things are due on Demo Day:

1. Live Demo (2 min)
A rehearsed, working demo of your robotвҖҷs key interaction. Not a video вҖ” the robot itself, running live.






ImportantMurphyвҖҷs Law



Something will go wrong during the demo. Prepare for it: have a backup power source, know how to reboot quickly, and have a вҖңgraceful failureвҖқ script ready (вҖңLet me restart вҖ” while it boots, let me tell you what it just didвҖҰвҖқ).





2. Academic Poster (A1 or A0)
Your poster is a standalone artifact that tells the full story of your project. It should be readable without you standing next to it.
Poster structure:



Section
Content
Size




Title
Robot name + one-line description
Large


The Problem
Who is the user? WhatвҖҷs the need?
Medium


Our Solution
What the robot does and how
Medium


Technology
Hardware stack + LLM integration diagram
Medium


Design Process
Key decisions and iterations
Medium


Results
User test findings, what worked
Medium


Lessons Learned
What youвҖҷd do differently
Small


QR Code
Links to GitHub repo or demo video
Small









TipPoster design advice




One key image per section вҖ” photos of your robot, circuit, or users testing it
Limit text: captions and bullets, not paragraphs
Use your brand colors consistently
Print at least one day early вҖ” printers break






3. Pitch Presentation (5 min + Q&A)
A 5-minute structured pitch to university guests.
Recommended structure:
00:00 вҖ” Hook (30 sec): Open with the user's problem. Make it vivid.
00:30 вҖ” Solution (1 min): Introduce the robot. What does it do?
01:30 вҖ” Demo (2 min): Live demonstration. Let the robot speak.
03:30 вҖ” Under the hood (45 sec): How it works вҖ” ESP32, ESP-Claw, LLM.
04:15 вҖ” What we learned (30 sec): One design insight, one technical lesson.
04:45 вҖ” Close (15 sec): Who should use this. Why it matters.






NoteQ&A is part of the evaluation



Guests will ask real questions. DonвҖҷt be defensive вҖ” say вҖңthatвҖҷs a good question, hereвҖҷs what we foundвҖқ or вҖңwe donвҖҷt know yet, but our hypothesis isвҖҰвҖқ Intellectual honesty is respected more than false confidence.

title: Week 4 вҖ” Pitch It вҳ…
text:
Sessions
вҶ’ View period-by-period schedule

Session 7 вҖ” Monday В· Presentation Coaching & Final Prep
Prof.В Zhou runs two periods of presentation coaching in the morning. The afternoon is for hardening everything.
Agenda: - Guest: Prof.В Zhou вҖ” presentation coaching: story, structure & slide design (Part 1) - Guest: Prof.В Zhou вҖ” presentation coaching: Q&A prep & live feedback (Part 2) - Poster review: content, layout & print finalization В· Shimon + Daniel - Demo hardening: run-throughs, backup power, reboot plan В· Shimon + Daniel - Full pitch rehearsal with instructor feedback (5 min + Q&A) В· Shimon + Daniel - Last design polish В· submit poster print file В· Daniel



Session 8 вҖ” Wednesday В· вҳ… Demo Day
The main event. University guests, all teams presenting simultaneously (poster session format).
Schedule: - Morning (periods 1вҖ“2): open preparation, last fixes, rehearsal - Period 3вҖ“4: setup вҖ” tables, displays, power, poster mounting - Period 5: вҳ… Poster session & live robot demos (guests circulate freely, ~45 min) - Period 6: вҳ… Full-group pitches вҖ” each team 5 min + 5 min Q&A






TipDuring the poster session



At least one team member should always be at the poster to engage with guests. Rotate so everyone gets to walk around and see other teamsвҖҷ work.




вң“ Final Deliverables (Demo Day)

Working robot demo вҖ” live, rehearsed, reliable
Printed A1/A0 poster вҖ” follows the poster structure above
5-minute pitch вҖ” all team members participate
GitHub repo вҖ” clean code, README, photos, short video
Reflection document (1 page) вҖ” what you built, what you learned, whatвҖҷs next

title: Week 4 вҖ” Pitch It вҳ…
text:
Resources for Week 4
Poster Design

#BetterPoster Template вҖ” Mike Morrison (OSF) вҖ” the influential вҖңsingle big finding in plain languageвҖқ poster format, free to download
Ten Simple Rules for a Good Poster Presentation вҖ” PLOS вҖ” peer-reviewed, evidence-based rules for effective scientific poster communication
Creating a Scientific Poster вҖ” NIH вҖ” layout ratios, typography, and content hierarchy from NIHвҖҷs training office
How to Make a Scientific Poster вҖ” FourWaves вҖ” practical step-by-step with software recommendations

Pitching & Presentations

How to Build Your Seed Round Pitch Deck вҖ” Y Combinator вҖ” YCвҖҷs canonical slide-by-slide pitch guide (free)
How to Design a Better Pitch Deck вҖ” Y Combinator вҖ” why plain, substance-first decks beat flashy ones
Public Speaking Tips вҖ” Toastmasters International вҖ” body language, vocal variety, managing nerves, and audience engagement
Top TED Talks on Public Speaking вҖ” TED вҖ” learn from master communicators by watching them



  
    рҹҸҶ Four weeks. One product. All yours.
  
  
    Everything you conceive, design, build, and pitch in Maker-X is your work.
    Take it further after the course ends.
  

вҶҗ Week 3 вҖ” Build

title: Resources
text:
// CORE TECHNOLOGY В В·В  ж ёеҝғжҠҖжңҜ
Everything your team needs to build an AI-powered robot. Every team gets the same foundation вҖ” what you build on top of it is entirely yours.

title: Resources
text:
Hardware

ESP32-Based Boards
The ESP32 is the brain of your robot: dual-core 240 MHz processor, built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, and enough compute for real-time AI interaction.
We use ESP32-based development boards (e.g.В ESP32-S3, ESP32-CAM variants depending on your project).
Specifications relevant to this course: - Dual-core Xtensa LX7 @ 240 MHz - 512 KB SRAM + up to 16 MB Flash (module-dependent) - Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n (essential for LLM API calls) - I2C, SPI, UART, PWM, ADC вҖ” all the interfaces youвҖҷll need
Resources: - Official ESP32 Datasheet - ESP-IDF Documentation - PlatformIO ESP32 platform


Peripherals
Teams will work with a subset of these components depending on their concept:



Component
Purpose
Interface




OLED display (SSD1306, 128Г—64)
Text / emotion display
I2C


LCD display (ST7789, 240Г—240)
Color display
SPI


Microphone (INMP441)
Voice input
I2S


Speaker + amplifier (MAX98357)
Voice output
I2S


Camera (OV2640)
Visual awareness
SCCB/I2C + parallel


Servo / DC motor + driver
Movement, expression
PWM


Ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04)
Distance / presence
GPIO


Capacitive touch sensor
Touch interaction
GPIO/ADC



3D Fabrication:
Your robotвҖҷs enclosure will be 3D-modeled and printed. The physical form is part of the design вҖ” it communicates personality before the robot says a word.
Recommended modeling tools: TinkerCAD (beginner), Fusion 360 (advanced, free for students).

title: Resources
text:
Frameworks

ESP-Claw
ESP-Claw is a purpose-built firmware framework for ESP32 AI robots. It abstracts the complex integration of voice, display, and LLM calls so you can focus on your idea rather than low-level plumbing.
What ESP-Claw handles for you: - Wi-Fi connection management - HTTP/HTTPS requests to LLM APIs - Audio capture and playback pipeline - Display rendering (text, icons, animations) - OTA firmware updates
Espressif engineers will give a guest lecture on ESP32 hardware and the ESP-Claw framework in Week 1.
вҶ’ ESP-Claw Documentation


OpenRouter
OpenRouter is a unified API gateway for large language models. Instead of integrating each model vendor separately, you write one API call and can switch between Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude, and dozens of others by changing a single parameter.
Why this matters for hardware: - Your ESP32 speaks to one endpoint вҖ” simpler networking code - Test multiple models without re-engineering your firmware - Manage costs across models in one dashboard - Automatic fallbacks if a model is unavailable
# Example: same call, different model
curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENROUTER_KEY" \
  -d '{"model": "qwen/qwen-2.5-72b-instruct", "messages": [...]}'

# Switch to DeepSeek: change one field
  -d '{"model": "deepseek/deepseek-chat", "messages": [...]}'
вҶ’ OpenRouter Documentation В· Model list & pricing

title: Resources
text:
Large Language Models

Models weвҖҷll work with
Your robotвҖҷs intelligence comes from LLMs. WeвҖҷll use models accessible via OpenRouter:








Model
Strengths
Use when




Qwen 2.5
Excellent Chinese + English, fast
Bilingual interactions, speed matters


DeepSeek Chat
Strong reasoning, very cost-effective
Logic-heavy interactions, budget-conscious


Claude (Haiku / Sonnet)
Best instruction-following, nuanced responses
Complex personality, careful tone



Choosing a model for your robot: - Response speed matters on embedded hardware вҖ” test latency with your Wi-Fi - Cost matters if your demo runs many queries вҖ” check OpenRouterвҖҷs per-token pricing - Language quality matters for personality вҖ” do a side-by-side with your system prompt






TipModel selection isnвҖҷt permanent



Your system prompt and model choice are both adjustable throughout the course. Design your firmware so swapping models is a one-line change.

title: Resources
text:
AI Coding Tools
Writing embedded firmware is faster with AI assistance. You will use:



Claude Code
AnthropicвҖҷs AI coding agent in your terminal. Understands your full codebase and can write, debug, and explain C++ / Arduino / ESP-IDF code.
Requires an Anthropic Pro subscription ($20/month вҖ” check if your institution has access).
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
вҶ’ Claude Code docs




GitHub Copilot
In-editor AI code completion and generation inside VS Code. Excellent for boilerplate, API calls, and explaining unfamiliar code.
вҶ’ GitHub Copilot for students (free via GitHub Education)




VS Code + PlatformIO + ESP-IDF
The recommended IDE setup for ESP32 firmware development. PlatformIO handles board configuration, dependency management, and flashing. ESP-IDF is the official Espressif framework.
This setup is optional вҖ” Arduino IDE also works for getting started.
вҶ’ PlatformIO for VS Code В· ESP-IDF docs




Blender
Free, open-source 3D creation suite used for sculpting and modeling organic robot enclosures. When your robotвҖҷs form needs something beyond simple geometric shapes, Blender is the right tool.
вҶ’ blender.org (free)

title: Resources
text:
Version Control
All teams must maintain a GitHub repository throughout the course.
# Suggested repo structure
your-robot/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ firmware/          # ESP32 code
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ src/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ platformio.ini
в”ңв”Җв”Җ design/            # 3D files, schematics, mockups
в”ңв”Җв”Җ research/          # User interview notes, concept brief
в”ңв”Җв”Җ poster/            # Poster source file
в””в”Җв”Җ README.md          # Project description + demo photo/video






Note



Your GitHub repo is a deliverable. Commit regularly. Write commit messages that would make sense to a stranger.
